Name: Sarbanes-Oxley Act
Source: U.S. Congress - Public Company Accounting Oversight

Controls.Description: Corporate responsibility for financial reports - CEO/CFO certify accuracy of financial statements
Officers certify they have reviewed the report and it does not contain untrue statements or material omissions
Officers are responsible for establishing and maintaining internal controls and have evaluated effectiveness within 90 days
Officers have disclosed all significant deficiencies and material weaknesses in internal controls to auditors and audit committee
Management assessment of internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR)
Management must establish and maintain an adequate internal control structure and procedures for financial reporting
External auditor must attest to and report on management's assessment of internal controls
Real-time issuer disclosures of material changes in financial condition or operations on a rapid and current basis
Disclosures shall include trend and qualitative information and graphic presentations as the SEC prescribes
Criminal penalties for altering, destroying, mutilating, concealing or falsifying documents to obstruct investigation
Audit work papers and review records must be retained for a period of 5 years from end of fiscal period
Whistleblower protection - no retaliation against employees who report fraud or violations to law enforcement or Congress
Corporate responsibility for financial reports - criminal penalties for CEO/CFO who knowingly certify false statements
Periodic reports must fully comply with Securities Exchange Act and fairly present financial condition and results of operations
Retaliation against informants - criminal penalties for knowingly retaliating against any person providing truthful information to law enforcement
Logical access controls - ensure only authorized users can access financial systems and data
User access provisioning - formal process for granting, modifying and revoking access to financial applications and data
Periodic access reviews - regular review and recertification of user access rights to financial systems
Privileged access management - restrict and monitor administrative and elevated access to financial systems
Authentication controls - password policies, multi-factor authentication, session management for financial systems
Segregation of duties - prevent any single individual from having conflicting access rights that could enable fraud
Change management controls - ensure changes to financial applications are authorized, tested and properly implemented
Change authorization - formal approval process for changes to applications, databases and infrastructure supporting financial reporting
Change testing - changes must be tested in a non-production environment before deployment to production
Separation of environments - development, testing and production environments are logically separated
IT operations controls - ensure reliable processing, monitoring, backup and recovery of financial systems
Job scheduling and monitoring - batch processing and automated jobs are monitored for successful completion and failures are investigated
Backup and recovery - regular backups of financial data and systems with tested recovery procedures
Incident management - process for identifying, logging, resolving and escalating IT incidents affecting financial systems
System development lifecycle controls - ensure new applications and systems supporting financial reporting are properly designed, developed, tested and approved
